Q1: I tried to keep things organized, but honestly with six online courses it was a constant juggling act. I'd usually set aside time in the mornings before my day got busy, listening to lecture recordings while doing other tasks. I'm 26, so I've got more responsibilities than younger students, and I had to fit studying around everything else. I aimed for about eight hours a week, but those hours were scattered across different days depending on what was most urgent.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and what was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be consistent, logging in every other day, but other weeks everything would pile up and I'd have to cram. I tried to stick to a morning routine when possible because that's when my mind is freshest. The unpredictability of my schedule meant I couldn't always study at the same times, which was frustrating.
Q3: Usually it was either an upcoming deadline or a notification that something new had been posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were discussion posts from my peers that I needed to respond to. The peer support aspect of this course was actually helpful for keeping me accountable. If I knew someone was expecting a response from me, that would motivate me to log in even when I wasn't feeling it.
Q4: There were a few weeks where checking the LMS became almost automatic, like first thing in the morning with my coffee. That happened when I got into a rhythm with the discussion groups and actually felt connected to other students. But I'll be honest, that routine broke down when things got stressful or when I felt overwhelmed by the workload. It never became truly habitual for the whole semester.
Q5: The audio and video content was most valuable to me because I learn best by listening. I'd play lecture recordings multiple times, sometimes at different speeds, until concepts clicked. The discussion forums were also useful because hearing different perspectives from peers helped me understand material I was struggling with. When classmates explained things in their own words, it often made more sense than the formal lectures.
Q6: I tended to skip over the reading-heavy PDFs and text-based supplementary materials. With my auditory learning style, I just couldn't absorb information as well from reading compared to listening. I'd skim them if absolutely necessary, but I definitely didn't engage deeply with written resources. Some of the optional practice quizzes I also ignored because I was already stretched thin with six courses.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to what would be on the exam or assignments. Also, anything that involved interaction with peers felt valuable because it broke the isolation of online learning. If something helped me understand a difficult concept through discussion or explanation, that was time well spent. Activities that felt like busywork with no clear purpose were harder to motivate myself to complete.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. With my workload across six courses, I had to prioritize required work first. Sometimes I'd look at optional materials and genuinely want to engage with them, but the anxiety of falling behind on mandatory assignments would pull me away. Required activities had a deadline attached, which gave them urgency that optional ones lacked.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that wouldn't show up in the LMS. I'd download audio lectures and listen to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also had study sessions with a few peers from the course where we'd meet up and discuss material together offline. Those conversations were some of my most productive learning moments, but they're completely invisible to any tracking system.
Q10: There were many times when I'd listen to a downloaded lecture recording three or four times while walking or exercising, really processing the information, but the LMS would only show one download. Or I'd be reviewing notes I'd taken by hand, connecting concepts across different modules, doing real cognitive work that left no digital trace. My offline study time was probably equal to or greater than what I did directly on the platform.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my standard approach. I'd access a video lecture once to download it or take notes, then I'd revisit those materials repeatedly offline. The LMS would show minimal engagement, but I was actually deeply engaged with the content in my own way. It was more convenient for me to have materials saved locally where I could access them without internet or during my commute.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engagement became really hard, usually when multiple deadlines from different courses converged. My anxiety would spike, and paradoxically, that made it harder to actually sit down and do the work. I'd feel this overwhelming sense of having too much to do, which sometimes led to a kind of paralysis. During those times, even logging into the LMS felt daunting because I'd see all the things I was behind on.
Q13: The workload across six courses was genuinely overwhelming at times. Fatigue was a real issue, especially later in the semester when everything piled up. When I was exhausted, I'd log into the LMS but my brain wasn't really processing anything. Competing responsibilities outside of school also meant that sometimes the LMS got neglected even when I knew I should be engaging more actively with it.
Q14: Definitely. There were times I'd have a video playing but my mind was elsewhere, worrying about other assignments or life stuff. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing what people were saying. My anxiety sometimes made it hard to focus even when I was technically present on the platform. Those sessions probably counted as engagement in the data, but they weren't productive learning moments.
Q15: I postponed exam preparation more than I should have, which probably explains my low exam score despite completing most assignments. The anxiety around exams made me avoid thinking about them until the last minute. I knew studying for exams was crucial, but the pressure felt paralyzing. I'd tell myself I'd get to it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with its own urgent tasks.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary driver for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active, completing assignments and catching up on materials. Without a deadline, I struggled to prioritize LMS engagement over other demands. This meant my activity was very uneven, with bursts of engagement followed by quiet periods. It wasn't the healthiest pattern, but it's how I survived six courses.
Q17: Feedback had a significant impact on my engagement. When I received encouraging feedback from peers or instructors, it motivated me to stay involved in discussions. Constructive criticism helped me understand where to focus my efforts. However, when feedback was delayed or minimal, I felt disconnected and uncertain about whether I was on the right track. The peer support intervention actually helped because I got more regular feedback from classmates.
Q18: The course structure with its multiple modules and regular discussion requirements created a framework that kept me somewhat on track. Having peer interaction built into the structure was helpful for someone like me who benefits from collaborative learning. However, the sheer volume of content across six courses meant I sometimes had to make strategic choices about what to engage with deeply versus what to skim.
Q19: The audio and video content made engagement easier because it matched my learning style. The discussion forums were well-designed for peer support, which I appreciated. What made engagement harder was the volume of reading materials and the lack of flexibility in some deadlines. Also, the exam format didn't play to my strengths as an auditory learner, which contributed to my lower exam performance despite decent assignment completion.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking the LMS but can't bring themselves to actually submit work. They might be reading, watching, participating in discussions, but then freeze when it comes to high-stakes submissions. Or they could be like me, where the actual productive work happens offline after downloading materials. High activity doesn't always translate to completed deliverables, especially under stress.
Q21: That resonates with my experience to some extent. I completed nearly 90% of my assignments despite relatively low LMS engagement levels. A lot of my real learning happened offline, through downloaded materials and peer study sessions. Someone could absolutely learn effectively while showing minimal platform activity if they're doing substantial work outside the system. The LMS only captures one dimension of engagement.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accesses materials and how often they participate in visible activities like discussions. They capture patterns of deadline-driven behavior pretty well. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement during a session, and the mental state of the student. They can't tell if someone watched a video attentively or had it playing in the background while stressed about something else.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, asking questions, making connections, and feeling invested in understanding. It's not just about logging in or completing tasks, but about mental presence and curiosity. For me, engagement often looks like discussing concepts with peers, replaying lectures until something clicks, and applying ideas to real situations. It's an internal state that external metrics can only partially capture.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my offline study sessions with peers, which were some of my most valuable learning experiences. It wouldn't capture the emotional journey of managing high anxiety while staying resilient and pushing through. It can't show that despite failing the final grade, I actually learned a lot and grew as a person through this course. The sadness I feel isn't about lack of effort; it's about a system that couldn't fully see or accommodate how I learn best.
